steps.dialogue: This was a good session.  [event:690cf13d7fae2f1c] When you had to figure out whether [event:690cf193bd71c1c5] one-sixth or one-third was bigger, you had the tools to  [event:690cf2015fe234ae] figure it out.

The ability to identify and work with unit fractions is a useful skill to build. When you see any [event:690cf3b1c9ca7227]fraction with 1 on top, you can now visualize it, [event:690cf40f74227d68]compare it to others, and explain WHY it works the way it does. That's advanced thinking.
We practiced [event:690cf5a7844ad936] that [event:690cf63a17b84677] ALL fractions are built from unit fraction blocks.
And this thinking translates to the real world! You saw that when we imagined using fractions to evenly divide pizzas and cake.

So before we end,[event:690cfa288b6b7fe3]  please take a moment to think about one thing you're proud to have learned in this lesson. Maybe it was the [vocab]inverse relationship[/vocab] between fractions like [event:690cfa88f46ccbda] one-fourth and one-eighth. Maybe it's that seeing a '1' as the top number [event:690cfbf670acc594]  of a fraction tells us that its a unit fraction.
Or maybe, it's just learning how to fairly divide a pizza with your friends. Whatever it is, hold onto the feeling of understanding something deeply.
Next time we'll take these unit fraction building blocks and start combining them to build OTHER fractions.
Your hard work today shows that you're ready for what's next. We'll take those next steps together.
